soil_type: well-drained loam, tolerant of poor soils
toxicity_notes: Edible fruit; no significant toxicity to humans or pets in normal consumption. Leaves used in folk medicine.
native_regions: Native to Mexico, Central America, and northern South America; invasive/naturalized in Hawaii, the Galapagos, southern Africa, New Zealand, and many Pacific islands.

soil_type: well-drained, adaptable to various soil types
toxicity_notes: Edible fruit; no significant toxicity in normal consumption for humans. Generally regarded as non-toxic, though not specifically studied for pets.
native_regions: Native to South America (Brazil, Uruguay, Paraguay, Argentina); documented as invasive in Florida, Hawaii, and other subtropical/tropical regions worldwide.

toxicity_notes: Leaves and essential oil are toxic to cats and dogs if ingested; can cause GI upset and CNS depression. Not generally toxic to humans but oil is toxic if ingested undiluted.
native_regions: Native to Australia (Tasmania and southeastern Victoria). Widely documented as invasive/aggressive introduced species in California, Portugal, Spain, South Africa, Chile, and other parts of South America.

soil_type: tolerant of a wide range including heavy clay and seasonally waterlogged soils
toxicity_notes: Leaves and essential oil toxic to cats and dogs if ingested. Not typically toxic to humans in normal contact.
native_regions: Native to Australia (widespread along inland watercourses in all mainland states). Documented as invasive/naturalized in South Africa, California, and parts of the Mediterranean.

soil_type: deep, well-drained, moist fertile soils preferred
toxicity_notes: Leaves and essential oil toxic to cats and dogs if ingested. Not typically toxic to humans in normal contact.
native_regions: Native to eastern Australia (coastal Queensland and New South Wales). Naturalized/invasive in South Africa, Brazil, and other plantation regions of South America and Africa.

toxicity_notes: Not significantly toxic; berries are edible and used in traditional Mediterranean cuisine and liqueurs (e.g. mirto). Essential oil can cause irritation in concentrated form.
native_regions: Native to the Mediterranean region (Southern Europe, North Africa, Middle East/Western Asia).

soil_type: adaptable, tolerates wet and periodically flooded soils
toxicity_notes: Edible fruit; generally safe for humans in normal consumption. Seeds used medicinally; no major documented pet toxicity, though excess seed consumption is not recommended.
native_regions: Native to the Indian subcontinent and South Asia (India, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Myanmar). Documented as invasive in Florida and various Pacific islands (e.g. Hawaii).

soil_type: rich, well-drained volcanic or loamy soils
toxicity_notes: Clove buds are safe as a culinary spice; however, concentrated clove/eugenol essential oil can be toxic (hepatotoxic, GI irritant) to humans and especially toxic to cats and dogs if ingested undiluted.
native_regions: Native to the Maluku Islands (Moluccas), Indonesia.

soil_type: moist, well-drained, tolerates seasonally waterlogged soils
toxicity_notes: Tea tree oil is TOXIC TO CATS AND DOGS if ingested or applied undiluted, causing tremors, ataxia, and CNS depression; also toxic to humans if ingested. Not a broadly documented ecological invader outside Australia (unlike the related Melaleuca quinquenervia, which is a major Florida invasive).
native_regions: Native to Australia, specifically the coastal areas of northern New South Wales.

soil_type: well-drained, tolerant of a wide range of soils including clay
toxicity_notes: Generally considered non-toxic to humans and pets (ASPCA lists related Callistemon/bottlebrush as non-toxic); no significant documented toxicity.
native_regions: Native to Australia (New South Wales and Queensland).

soil_type: well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soils
toxicity_notes: Edible fruit; no significant toxicity in normal consumption for humans or documented major pet toxicity.
native_regions: Native to the highlands of southern Brazil, eastern Paraguay, Uruguay, and northern Argentina.

toxicity_notes: Fruit (allspice) is a safe culinary spice in normal use; concentrated essential oil (high eugenol content) can be irritating/toxic if ingested in large amounts by humans or pets.
native_regions: Native to Central America and the Caribbean (Mexico, Guatemala, Belize, Honduras, Jamaica, Cuba).

toxicity_notes: Edible fruit; no significant toxicity in normal consumption. Assessed as non-invasive/low risk in Florida (UF/IFAS) and not known to be invasive in Hawaii.
native_regions: Native to southeastern Brazil (Minas Gerais, Rio de Janeiro, São Paulo, Goiás), with related range extending to Bolivia, Paraguay, Uruguay, and northern Argentina.

soil_type: well-drained soils of seasonally dry tropical/subtropical forest
toxicity_notes: Edible fruit traditionally consumed and used in folk medicine; no significant documented toxicity in normal consumption.
native_regions: Native to South America: eastern Bolivia to southeastern and southern Brazil, Paraguay, and northeastern Argentina.

toxicity_notes: No significant documented toxicity; used in traditional medicine and for essential oil production. No confirmed reports of pet/human toxicity found.
native_regions: Native to South America: Brazil, Bolivia, Peru, Ecuador, Paraguay, Uruguay, and northern Argentina.
